speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about what happened when you ran away.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is Febuary the 4th, 1996. This is Freda Glezer of Freda in Cape Town, tape 2.

speaker: interviewee
text: How many tapes you need? Four?

speaker: interviewer
text: Freda, you ran away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You took some money. And you went to the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I passed the border. And I started to go villages. And the people in Russia say, why did you come? We haven't got bread. We haven't got nothing. You-- why did you come? You know? It's too [INAUDIBLE] ourselves. We haven't got nothing. We didn't believe. And we went walking, and walking, and walking. Still we came to Zhitomir. In Zhitomir I had a friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away is Zhitomir?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's far. We walked about four or five days. We walked. And we-- so we came to Zhitomir. And I went to my friend that this was a friend. It was a Jewish man. He was in the army, a politruk, that means a politic-- a-- he used to-- to speak to people. They must be Communists, you know. Politruk they calls him in Russia. And he was staying in this room, what I told you, they took away from us the room. And he came. And he asked--

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Russians moved in, and they took away a room, he was the man that put you in the room. So that's how you got to know him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I got to know him. And he asked us if he can bring his wife and a child. And they were spending nearly a summer. So he-- when he left, he gave us the address that maybe sometimes he would want to come too. That was before that happened, because he went away before it started the war-- much before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to look him up.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to look him up. And I did spend a few days there, very nice people, very charming people. And the bomb-- they started bombing in Zhitomir. But started so heavy that I didn't go back. I don't know what happened to them. I didn't learn nothing. So they-- they-- a few of us kept together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these all people from your-- from-- your village, from Dubno?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Dubno, yes, from Dubno. And we went through the forest. We did go day and night, day and night. At night, we used to sleep, keeping hands, because we didn't even-- we didn't have sense to buy a packet of matches in the forest because maybe we-- you know, to put on a match to see if somebody wants to go to make something, we couldn't. Everybody had to go out together because it was so dark. In the daytime, they used to bomb only the railways and the towns, but not the trees. You see, in forests, you could go. You could just go. So we went still Kiev.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what we did eat. I can't tell you, because already my feet were so swollen and everything was impossible. Anyway, we came to Kiev. And in Kiev was a very large Jewish community. They straight away asked us if we are [? the ?] Jewish people. They took us. We had a wash. We had a little bit to eat, and then they say it's a school in-- there's two schools where they take all the immigrants and say, at least you will sleep in a-- not in field. You will sleep in a-- in a room, on a floor somewhere. And then maybe we'll go trains. We'll go trains. And you will come somewhere. You must come somewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was-- they was very right We were three, four days. And then went-- that is not trains from passenger, but train from goods and from cattle, you know? That was the trains. And we-- we went by this train till Kharkov. Where is Kharkov?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kharkov is a very big town. It also-- it still-- still belong-- it's still Ukraine, you know? Dubno belonged to Ukraine. It's still, you know, the part of-- it's a very rich part and a very beautiful part. So I went to-- to Kharkov. We did go by these trains. They used to throw us out. If they need something to take goods or something, go out. We used to sit in a field and just wait-- wait two days, three days without water. You have food or no food. We used to wait. Another train came and took us another day somewhere-- slept somewhere till we came to Kharkov. And when we came to Kharkov, this was organized-- more organized already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went by train to Kharkov.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, by train--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you'd have to get out and sit in the field for two or three days before you'd catch another train.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know if we will come or we will not come. But it was not going back because it was nowhere. We-- we knew the bombs had gone to Kharkov already. The people did suffer already. You couldn't come in and shop. There was nothing in the shops, nothing, absolutely like my late mother used to say, you can buy for the ruble nothing, but keep it. After this, we could buy when we came to Tashkent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you still have money with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had some money. I gave a lot of money. You know, we-- we asked them how to from-- from this-- through the forest. We didn't know how to go. When you ask somebody, you had to pay him something, you know, to take you a little bit, to show you the way. Because no map, nothing, you're not-- you're going. You're going blind. You didn't where you're going. But you're going. Kharkov was organized already. Kharkov was organized-- more trains and more things. And they say to us, we will take us-- the trains will take some people to Tashkent. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Tashkent in Asia. We will go to Tashkent. It's no-- no choice. When we came to this train, then I met a Russian woman with two children, two small children. And her husband was officer in the army. He put her on the train and straightaway said maybe you can help my wife with the-- the whole group [? of us, ?] when she will need some bottle or something for the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: We traveled still Tashkent from Kharkov-- still Tashkent. It take maybe two weeks. I don't-- I don't remember how long. I know we-- the train used to stop. Then we got both of them. We got that for two days, three days, you know? It was a very big thing to get a little bit of hot water for the children. They knew how to travel because they had kettles and pots with them, everything. You know? We-- we did have nothing. We didn't know. We couldn't have the hot water, because you can't take hot in your hand and to your mouth. You had to struggle like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we went to Tashkent. In Tashkent, there was a big, like a garden, a big garden. And everyone sat there-- every-- people from all over the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a park?

speaker: interviewee
text: A park. We did sleep there. We did sit there. We were there a few good days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- how did you get food all this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. We used to get-- you know, people used-- that what I said. We used to buy something that must cost 10 cents. You paid 100 rubles-- you know, a little bit, to eat something. And then it was a few days, nearly more than a week, in Tashkent. In Tashkent, was-- and she went straight away, away. [? Galia ?] went straight away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is [? Galia? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: These friends, these Russian friends, because she's officer's wife. So she asked them with two small children. And she had a permit to go to her sister. Her sister was in Namangan. And said--

speaker: interviewer
text: Her sister was where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Namangan.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just like you say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Namangan.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And she says to me, Freda, listen, I'm going to my sister. My sister works in a very good place. And that is in Namangan. And maybe you will meet me. If you meet me, maybe I will able to help you. We are going-- the whole company. So we came to Namangan.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Namangan?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same, by train. She went away before. In Tashkent, we came out, [? one ?] from the Russian people. In Tashkent, it was a comedy. You know. And if you had a few rubles, you could go in. And they drink tea. You know, the Uzbek, they drink black tea.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Namangan is in Uzbekistan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Uzebekistan. Tashkent is [? in ?] Uzbekistan. I'm speaking still about Tashkent. I used to say, let us sleep on the floor. We were so sick and-- sick. On the ground we used to sleep, not-- not a blanket, nothing to put-- just like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still in the same clothes that you were when you left home?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same clothes the whole time, same clothes-- full of lice, full of dirt. People didn't want to come near you. You know? Then you-- I used to give him something.

speaker: interviewer
text: To who?

speaker: interviewee
text: To these cafes where they drink the-- the black tea to give us a little bit of hot tea, something. They used to give us. But I-- i think that they used to throw away. They were clean, still, you know? And we were so dirty and were looking so shocking I don't think that they used-- they-- they used to throw away everything that we touched. I'm telling you. I did see it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then afterwards, they did say one morning as is going a big train to Namangan. And there [INAUDIBLE] was this [INAUDIBLE] what town was this? [INAUDIBLE] in another town. And I asked the people-- my group if we will go to Namangan, because I-- I knew I would meet this Russian person. She told me exactly where and when. We came to Namangan. After maybe 12 days or 15 days, the same story. The train goes, doesn't go. You know? It is not questions-- in Russia, it is not questions because for questions, you can to-- to jail for a good few years. It just one lesson we had and-- and was explained to us-- no complaints, no telling, and not asking for nothing. Just that is that. We came to-- we came to Namangan. Again, we haven't got where to be. We were also on the streets. And then they asked us, who wants to work? Came one that he needs people. So we went to work. But dried fruit-- you know, to clean dried-- to make-- they make a lot-- Namangan, Asia has a lot of wonderful fruit-- wonderful fruit. So we worked there maybe eight days or 10 days or 12 days. By now, had you had the opportunity to wash and clean your clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not yet. We still were-- then we asked--

speaker: interviewer
text: I hope they let you wash your hands before you handled the dried fruit.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I did ask that was one. But it was a judge. And I said, how can you arrange us to have a-- a job? To have a job? He arranged us in this factory for the-- for the-- for the boots.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't tell me about boots. You were saying about dried fruit.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that is from the dried fruit. This man arranged us maybe they will take us to this factory to work. And then we did look for where to be-- where to sleep. We find a little [INAUDIBLE] here these people in [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] is good. She said you can't have it, because there are goats staying there. And their chickens were staying there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, we will clean up everything. Let us be-- let us go in, because was quite a big yard-- at least a yard we can wash ourselves in. And to go in, to be there, right? How much you want? She didn't even know what to say. First of all, she was scared, because in Russia to take money, they can go and report you or something. I say, I don't go. I don't know anybody here. I don't know nothing. I just ask you give us this place. I will clean it up, clean it up. There's nothing for us to clean.

speaker: interviewee
text: She gave us this place. And that we did stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was this goat shed and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That group of 10.

speaker: interviewee
text: 10-- 10 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Moved into the goat shed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and we had a yard. The yard was a very big helping. We could wash ourselves. We could sit a little bit-- you know, sit, not-- because you sit in the street, you don't know who will come next to you. And from there, I went to this goat [INAUDIBLE] that was a big-- big factory. Then the three of us-- we went to this factory. They-- some of the boys were straightaway mobilized.

speaker: interviewer
text: The boys in your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the group was straightaway mobilized in Namangan. We didn't see them anymore. And the few-- a few-- three of us, was to this factory. And a few of us went to another factory to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What factory was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: To make boots for the army, for the Red Army. You made boots from wool. With this part of your hand, you work. And it's a lot of strong things that you put like [INAUDIBLE] or something. I don't know how you-- you put it in these boots to make-- to make like a material, because it was plain wool, wool like you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that I did work a long time in this factory, a long, long time. We did work. And then Leningrad was [INAUDIBLE]. You remember Leningrad? And the people from Leningrad, a lot of people was emigrated to Namangan. They used to-- they used to come and tell us they eat mice. They used to eat mice. They-- these people were so absolutely hungry and dirty and everything. That-- it was besieged, Leningrad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. I did work the whole time in this factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: About six, seven months before the war finished. Then I-- I did-- I knew I've got a sister in Israel. I didn't know the address, nothing. Everything is gone. But one from our group, she-- she had a-- she had a brother in Israel-- she said, I remember the address. I'm going to write. And I will mention [? that you're with ?] us and give the name. Maybe, you know, in Israel when it's from the same towns, you straightaway know each other. And that's what my sister did get to know of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your friend wrote to her brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: To her brother. And her brother knew my sister. And he told her I'm in Namangan. Through the Joint, the American Joint, through [INAUDIBLE] you could send one parcel or two at the most. But you could send. I received after four or five weeks, I received a postcard from my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the postcard say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very carefully written-- I know you're there-- you know, a few words, because she knows Russian. And it's not-- she's not going to make me-- she knows which position. Then I came-- when the postcard came, it was such a beautiful postcard, and the paper as they didn't-- they never saw in Russia something like it. And everyone touched this card. I saw thought that is a joke. And they started asking me. I say, yes, my sister's in Israel. She-- she went away before the war. She went away before the war. She was given a certificate from the English government. She worked five years for the Zionist movement. Then she had the certificate to go there,  I hear-- went away three months, four months. I had an invoice that's I've got a parcel in the post office. And then it was very bad. You know, they used to sell the half a kilo of bread. But you have to go, yet, every day. They used to take it and sell it on the black market. And we didn't know-- 10 days, eight days is being without a piece of bread. And you could not complain, because you can't say nothing-- no bread. They say it's no bread today. There's no bread today. You just say you're going back. You already dying for-- for something, but nothing will help. So I received this from my sister that I've got a parcel. But I have to pay money to get the parcel. And you know, the-- all these people from Latvia, Lithuania-- it was-- together-- the Jewish people did keep together. Then I did tell one man that I knew from Riga. And I said to him, [? Luva ?] you know I have an invoice from-- for a parcel. But I haven't got a penny. He says, get it. Get the parcel. I will give you the money. I went. And I got the parcel. And they say sell every single thing, because you will have this soap for two weeks. And  if you will buy a special-- we had cards. You know, the system for bread. You will have a card.

speaker: interviewer
text: A ration card.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ration-- I will buy you another two cards. So you will have another kilo or half a kilo of bread. That will save you. And I sold every single thing from the parcel. I didn't leave even a piece of soap for me. And I-- and he bought me two bread cards. And this [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So for the food, and the soap, and the parcel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was sold.

speaker: interviewer
text: You bought two ration cards.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Extra ration cards.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but that was [? like gold, ?] you couldn't have. But on the black market, if you had money, you could-- it was [INAUDIBLE], knew how to get. They was making thousands. You know, they did-- I don't know from there they took it. Every single thing was there on the market. You only needed money. And it was the money--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was the black market.

speaker: interviewee
text: Black market. Yes. In the shops, nothing-- black market. And our wages was exactly for-- maybe for a week-- maybe for two weeks. There-- there there was nothing to buy. But  you had to have it, you see, for the-- to-- for everything-- for this rent, you had to have it. It was a terrible situation. A terrible-- it as so bad. And I still was so sorry what I did sell there, even the soap to wash myself or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I didn't tell you, I was in Namangan. And this little [INAUDIBLE] next to me was a family from Kiev. And he did work for the army, the man. A Jewish family was a daughter-- and these two-- the woman-- the man and the wife. And she used to say Freda, we've got vegetables. We get from the army vegetables. I used to run away. I was so ashamed. You know? Ashamed of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] You haven't got-- [DRINKS] [INAUDIBLE]. She didn't see me. She came and she said, I will never speak to you. You mustn't be like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, she did help us a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you not want to go to her because you were ashamed that you didn't have anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: They also didn't have. But they did have at least, they did have more bread. And the vegetables did give them. You see? Some carrots and cabbage-- and then they used to-- to cook and to make something. She used to say, I've got enough to divide it. We didn't have even what to cook. What can you cook? You haven't got a pot. You haven't got nothing. That was in Namangan.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- so what did you eat then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a half a kilo of bread with the factory. And then they used to make a soap from-- from tomatoes, from green tomatoes. And after this soup, we-- we used to, excuse me, sit in lavatory. And the lavatory wasn't a lavatory then. That was a problem. You didn't have even a little paper to wipe yourself. You didn't have nothing-- no newspaper, nothing, because that cost money.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to [? Galia? ?] Did you see [? Galia? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, [? Galia ?] met the four or five days after I was in Namangan. She gave me an address. I think she was very shocked to see me. I did look. But I cleaned up myself. He bought some from-- from sacks. You know, we made a little something to-- to wear-- you know, to-- to look--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you bought some sacks--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sacks--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and made yourself  some clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --made something to wear it. And we-- I washed my clothes. I did look better than I-- than [INAUDIBLE]. And her sister did help us a lot-- a lot. She used to give us bread once a week. You know, that was a very big thing, a bread. She used to give us a little bit of foil, something. You know, she did help us. They-- [? Galia ?] did work a milk-- you know how-- in a dairy. It looks like she did steal from the dairy, because she-- sometimes she used to give me a piece of butter or something. And they-- they caught her stealing. And it was a case. And she went to jail. I don't know for how long. Matter of fact, as I sent her parcels when I was in the DP camps. I sent to her a few parcels. I don't know if she received or not. But I knew. And her husband came. And he took away the children. There was then [? Ovgala. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: But she did help us a lot. You know, to get once week a bread, that was just like-- like to buy a-- a house, a flat, you know? Bread was a very big thing. And then I did get. So with your cards to have extra bread, it was really something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I had the extra bread, because it was weeks. But we didn't-- I divided it with these friends. And I had another one. It was three, four in this little thing. And you had to have it. Then I didn't tell you the story. Typhus was, you know, this-- the sickness typhus. And you had to go to hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: From lice? From lice.

speaker: interviewee
text: From lice, yes. You had to go to hospital. I didn't want her to go, because I knew-- you know what they--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is one of your friends who got it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I didn't want her to go, because I knew they used to throw out you in the night from the hospital, bury you alive. You know, you had high temperatures there. She had to go, because somebody reported that she has typhus. She has to go. I made arrangements there. In the night, I will come. She must get at-- we will get her. And we did get her. We got her back. We jumped out to get her back.

speaker: interviewer
text: You jumped out of the window?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the window, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you took her home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then I-- from our town, my town was one older person who knew me from home. But he was friends with a doctor from-- you know, in-- in Russia, every district has a doctor. So I did ask him to get me a certificate because I can't work any more in the factory, because I couldn't take it anymore. My hand was in blood already. You know, so-- so that's the end of  me. And I had arranged with somebody that would take me for work, privately. And the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sewing. She used to sew and sell it on the black market. And I used to help her with the sewing. She was from Bialystok, also a Jewish woman. How she did arrange herself, I don't know. She didn't work one day. She worked privately. She was a sick-- sickly woman. And I did get this certificate from this doctor. And they gave me for six months not to-- not to work in the factory. And my card for the bread still was-- I still could receive my bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- if  you didn't work, you didn't get a ration card?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then you don't get. But because I was-- had from the doctor this certificate, they asked me to-- for six months. After six months, it was already going into the war. Already, you know? We did hear already they took this place and that place. The German-- the Russian people that was from Moscow when they start to-- to-- to get on defeat, the Russian Army.

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that it will come to an end. And it's every day they took another town. And they took this town and that town. We did know that is going to be maybe end of the war. We knew [? that ?] [? there ?] [? was ?] concentration camps in-- in-- in Poland. We knew exactly, because everyone who came from the Russian Army, you know, they used to be sick-- shot in the leg-- shot in this. They used to be in the hospitals. And thousands of them-- thousands-- millions used to come without legs-- without their hands. The Russians--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Russians used to evacuate their wounded and put them in hospitals in--

speaker: interviewee
text: The hospitals in--

speaker: interviewer
text: --Uzbekistan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Uzbeskistan, because you couldn't put them in another one. There, it was quiet, you know? The-- you didn't have the bombs. You didn't have the-- then they did say, ooh, you don't know what's going on? You're lucky that you're here. They burned the people. The burned the people. It's-- they burned the people. You know, they didn't-- they couldn't explain to-- what they meant. But we knew this was a terrible thing. Everyone-- the ones that came, a Jewish soldier, they  would say, don't ask what's going on. It's-- they-- it's-- it's a place where by the thousands they were burning the Jewish people in thousands-- in thousands. And then start already going more and more to the borders. And then the war was finished. We still didn't know where we belonged-- where we were going. But we-- we didn't know nothing. The war was finished. I remember the night. They were celebrating. We knew we haven't got nothing to celebrate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went-- we went the second day to the factory. We went to the factory. They asked me, what you going to do? I said, I don't know. I want first to  write home to hear if maybe somebody is left. Then I want to see what everybody will do. The Polish people-- I had my passport till '39-- the Polish passport. You know? I belonged to Poland. It was still '39. They said till '39 who belonged to Poland will be able to go back to Poland. You had to know your language back. I did speak four solid years Russian. I had all forgot. Then once they think-- think hard will come the language back to you. I couldn't-- they asked me to speak something, I speak in Russian. I couldn't speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I-- after four or five days, I started thinking and how did go to school. And what I did right in school. And it came to me slowly, surely, came to me back the language. I had to register. And then I received a letter from my brother as he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the brother who had been in the potato field?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your brother say?

speaker: interviewee
text: He say it's very bad. He didn't want to tell me, as everybody was killed. But I did write then to my girlfriend-- this-- [INAUDIBLE] this Luba. To my-- and she-- she sent me a telegram.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was Luba?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends from school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is she the Czech girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czech girl. She-- she-- she did in telegram, tell me is-- is not-- you haven't got nobody. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [DRINKS] She asked me to come to her. And she said was a very honest person.

speaker: interviewer
text: A very?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very honest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Honest, mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said Freda, if you can help yourself, not to come up to see what's going on. It's bad. That was it. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Then I registered. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So she sent you a telegram saying that you've got nobody there. You've got nobody alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody. I can come. My-- my mother wants you. I want you. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [DRINKS] You see, I did grow with her. She was friends from [INAUDIBLE]. I used to go for every holiday to her. She used to come to us every single morning. They did want me. And I know, because I had afterwards a letter from her mother. She said you will be exactly like my daughter, [? Yakluba. ?] But I must tell you the truth. That it is very bad, very bad, the killings. [INAUDIBLE] right after the war, they are taking away everything they can get a hold of they are taking away. You will not be able to go into your house. They will kill you-- plain and simple.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I had a letter from my sister. She said don't move nowhere. You go with the people going. We-- they organized, trying to take the-- take a hold of these immigrants. You see? the Haganah did work already. They did work. The Jewish movement from Israel was already sent to Munich and all over, you know? Then we had to emigrate.
